# Configure service discovery

You have two options for service discovery:

- Consul service discovery, which requires access to a Consul cluster. Consul is
  the default option for service discovery.
- Nomad service discovery, which requires no additional infrastructure.

Refer to the [Service discovery in Nomad
documentation](/nomad/docs/networking/service-discovery) for a comparison of
Nomad and Consul service discovery options.

This guide uses an example job specification (jobspec) that deploys the
Countdash application to illustrate how to configure service discovery. The app
has a frontend web service that connects to a backend service. Each service
discovery section contains the complete job spec for you to deploy on your infrastructure.

## Consul service discovery

This section illustrates Consul service discovery using parts of the Countdash
example jobspec. The application has a frontend `countdash-web`
service that communicates with a backend `countdash-api` service.

You may view the complete job spec in the
[Countdash jobspec with Consul service discovery](##countdash-jobspec-with-consul-service-discovery) section.

### Declare the service provider

Consul is the default service provider, so you do not have to configure the `service`
block's [`provider` parameter](/nomad/docs/job-specification/service#provider).
This jobspec example explicitly declares `provider = "consul"`.

<CodeBlockConfig lineNumbers="true" highlight="4" filename="countdash.nomad.hcl">

```hcl
job "countdash" {
  group "countdash-api" {
    service {
      provider = "consul"
    }
  }
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>

### Configure health checks

Define health checks with the [`check`
block](/nomad/docs/job-specification/check) to make sure that the service
catalog only returns healthy instances. Health check configuration is the same
whether you use Consul service discovery or Nomad service discovery.

<Tabs>

<Tab heading="countdash-api">

<CodeBlockConfig lineNumbers="true" 
                 filename="countdash.nomad.hcl"
                 highlight="7-16" >

```hcl
job "countdash" {
  group "countdash-api" {
    service {
      name = "countdash-api"
      provider = "consul"
      
      check {
        name     = "Countdash API ready" 
        type     = "http"
        path     = "/actuator/health"
        interval  = "5s"
        timeout   = "5s"

        check_restart {
          limit = 0
        }
      }          
    }
  }
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>

</Tab>
<Tab heading="countdash-web">

<CodeBlockConfig lineNumbers="true" 
                 filename="countdash.nomad.hcl"
                 highlight="7-13" >

```hcl
job "countdash" {
  group "countdash-web" {
    service {
      name = "countdash-web"
      provider = "consul"

      check {
        name      = "Countdash web ready"
        type      = "http"
        path      = "/"
        interval  = "5s"
        timeout   = "5s"
      }      
    }
  }
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>
</Tab>
</Tabs>

### Configure access to other services

If you are using Consul for service discovery and have not enabled service mesh
features, use Consul DNS to discover services and nodes in the Consul catalog.
Do not use the client node addresses from Nomad for upstream service resolution.

To find a service in the Consul service catalog, use the following format:

```text
<service_name>.service.<consul_datacenter>.<consul_domain>
```

Consul's default datacenter is `dc1` and default domain is `consul`. Those
values are configurable, so verify those values with your Consul admin.

The `countdash-web` service needs to communicate with its backend
`countdash-api` service.  `COUNTING_SERVICE_URL` variable is populated by using
a Consul DNS lookup.

<CodeBlockConfig lineNumbers="true" 
                 filename="countdash.nomad.hcl"
                 highlight="6" >

```hcl
job "countdash" {
  group "countdash-web" {
    task "countdash-web" {
      ...
      env {
        COUNTING_SERVICE_URL = "http://countdash-api.service.dc1.consul:${var.countdash-api-port}"
        PORT="${var.countdash-web-port}"
      }
  }
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>

### Countdash jobspec with Consul service discovery

<details>
<summary style={{ cursor: "pointer" }}>
  Expand to review the full <code>countdash.nomad.hcl</code> jobspec.
</summary>

This jobspec deploys a job called "countdash" to a Nomad cluster
running on AWS. You may review the frontend and backend service code in the
[HashiCorp `demo-consul-101`
repository](https://github.com/hashicorp/demo-consul-101/blob/main/services/README.md).

The web application runs on port 9002, so make sure to open that port in your
AWS security group.

If you want to run the job on your localhost Linux Nomad and Consul Linux
installation, you should update each group's `service.address` parameter value
to `attr.unique.network.ip-address`.

<CodeBlockConfig lineNumbers="true" 
                 filename="countdash.nomad.hcl"
                 highlight="23,27-38,78,82-88,107-116">

```hcl
variable "countdash-api-port" {
  description = "Countdash API Port"
  default = 9001
}
variable "countdash-web-port" {
  description = "Countdash web port"
  default = 9002
}

# https://github.com/hashicorp/demo-consul-101/blob/master/services/dashboard-service/README.md

job "countdash" {

  group "countdash-api" {
    count = 1

    network {
      port "countdash-api" {
        static = var.countdash-api-port
      }
      dns {
      	servers = ["172.17.0.1"] 
      }
    }

    service {
      name = "countdash-api"
      provider = "consul"
      port = "countdash-api"      
      address  = attr.unique.platform.aws.local-ipv4

      check {
        name     = "Countdash API ready" 
        type     = "http"
        path     = "/actuator/health"
        interval  = "5s"
				timeout   = "5s"

        check_restart {
          limit = 0
        }
      }      
    }

    task "countdash-api" {
      driver = "docker"

      meta {
        service = "countdash-api"
      }

      config {
        image = "hashicorpdev/counter-api:v3"
        ports = ["countdash-api"]
       mount {
          type   = "bind"
          source = "local/application.properties"
          target = "/application.properties"
        }
      }
      template {
        data = "server.port=${var.countdash-api-port}"
        destination = "local/application.properties"
      }
      resources {
        memory = 500
      }
    }
  }

  group "countdash-web" {

    count = 1

    network {
      port "countdash-web" {
        static = var.countdash-web-port
      }
      dns {
      	servers = ["172.17.0.1"] 
      }
    }

    service {
      name = "countdash-web"
      provider = "consul"
      port = "countdash-web"
      address  = attr.unique.platform.aws.public-hostname
      check {
        name      = "Countdash web ready"
        type      = "http"
        path			= "/"
        interval  = "5s"
        timeout   = "5s"
      }
    }

    task "countdash-web" {
      driver = "docker"

      meta {
        service = "countdash-web"
      }
      env {
        COUNTING_SERVICE_URL = "http://countdash-api.service.dc1.consul:${var.countdash-api-port}"
        PORT="${var.countdash-web-port}"
      }

      config {
        image          = "hashicorpdev/counter-dashboard:v3"
        auth_soft_fail = true
        ports = ["countdash-web"]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>
</details>

### Find services in the Consul catalog

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="CLI">

User the [`consul catalog services` command](/consul/commands/catalog/services) to list services in the Consul catalog.

```shell-script
$ consul catalog services

consul
countdash-api
countdash-web
nomad
nomad-client
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="API">

Use the [`v1/catalog/services` API
endpoint](/consul/api-docs/catalog#list-services) to list services in the Consul
catalog.

Replace these placeholders:

- `<consul-http-address>:<port>`: Consul's public IP address and port, such as
  `http://13.58.60.124`
- `<consul-management-token>`: The value of your Consul token, which has
  appropriate privileges to query the Consul catalog. Refer to Consul's [HTTP API Structure guide](/consul/api-docs/api-structure) for token details.

```shell-script
curl --location 'http://<consul-http-address>:<port>/v1/catalog/services' \>
     --header 'X-Consul-Token: <consul-management-token>' 
```

Response is similar to the following:

```json
{
  "consul": [],
  "countdash-api": [],
  "countdash-web": [],
  "nomad": [],
  "nomad-client": []
}
```

</Tab>
</Tabs>


### Find your deployed service's public IP address

Use the Consul [`v1/catalog/service/:service_name>` API endpoint](/consul/api-docs/catalog#list-nodes-for-service) to find the public IP
address of your running `countdash-web` service.

Replace these placeholders:

- `<consul-http-address>:<port>`: Consul's public IP address and port, such as
  `http://13.58.60.124`
- `<consul-management-token>`: The value of your Consul token, which has
  appropriate privileges to query the Consul catalog. Refer to Consul's [HTTP API Structure guide](/consul/api-docs/api-structure) for token details.

```shell-script
curl --location 'http://<consul-http-address>:<port>/v1/catalog/service/countdash-web?passing' \>
     --header 'X-Consul-Token: <consul-management-token>'  | \
     jq -r '.[] | "\(.ServiceAddress):\(.ServicePort)"'
```

Output returns the public IPv4 address, which in this example is an AWS EC2
public IP and port.

```shell-script
ec2-3-145-209-63.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com:9002
```

## Nomad service discovery

This section illustrates Nomad service discovery using parts of the Countdash
example jobspec. The application has a frontend `countdash-web`
service that communicates with a backend `countdash-api` service.

You may view the complete job spec in the
[Countdash jobspec with Nomad service
discovery](#countdash-jobspec-with-nomad-service-discovery) section.

### Declare the service provider

Because Consul is the default service provider, you must configure the `service`
block's [`provider` parameter](/nomad/docs/job-specification/service#provider)
to use Nomad service discovery.  

<CodeBlockConfig lineNumbers="true" highlight="4" filename="countdash.nomad.hcl">

```hcl
job "countdash" {
  group "countdash-api" {
    service {
      provider = "nomad"
    }
  }
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>

### Configure health checks

Define health checks with the [`check`
block](/nomad/docs/job-specification/check) to make sure that the service
catalog only returns healthy instances. Health check configuration is the same
whether you use Consul service discovery or Nomad service discovery.

<Tabs>

<Tab heading="countdash-api">

<CodeBlockConfig lineNumbers="true" 
                 filename="countdash.nomad.hcl"
                 highlight="7-16" >

```hcl
job "countdash" {
  group "countdash-api" {
    service {
      name = "countdash-api"
      provider = "nomad"

      check {
        name     = "Countdash API ready" 
        type     = "http"
        path     = "/actuator/health"
        interval  = "5s"
        timeout   = "5s"

        check_restart {
          limit = 0
        }
      }          
    }
  }
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>

</Tab>
<Tab heading="countdash-web">

<CodeBlockConfig lineNumbers="true" 
                 filename="countdash.nomad.hcl"
                 highlight="7-13" >

```hcl
job "countdash" {
  group "countdash-web" {
    service {
      name = "countdash-web"
      provider = "nomad"

      check {
        name      = "Countdash web ready"
        type      = "http"
        path			= "/"
        interval  = "5s"
        timeout   = "5s"
      }      
    }
  }
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>
</Tab>
</Tabs>

### Configure access to other services

Use [`template` blocks](/nomad/docs/job-specification/template#nomad-services)
blocks with the Consul Template [`nomadService`
function](/consul/docs/reference/consul-template/go#service) to locate services
in the Nomad service discovery catalog. You may use the `template`  as a
configuration file or have its content loaded as environment variables to
configure connection information in applications.

The `countdash-web` service needs to communicate with its backend
`countdash-api` service. The template block code searches for
`countdash-api` and assigns the result to the `COUNTING_SERVICE_URL` variable.

<CodeBlockConfig lineNumbers="true" 
                 filename="countdash.nomad.hcl"
                 highlight="4-13" >

```hcl
job "countdash" {
  group "countdash-web" {
    task "countdash-web" {
      template {
        data = <<EOH
        BIND_ADDRESS = ":${var.countdash-api-port}"
{{ range nomadService "countdash-api" }}
COUNTING_SERVICE_URL = "http://{{ .Address }}:{{ .Port }}"
{{ end }}
EOH
        destination = "local/env.txt"
        env         = true
      }
    }
  }
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>

### Countdash jobspec with Nomad service discovery

<details>
<summary style={{ cursor: "pointer" }}>
  Expand to review the full <code>countdash.nomad.hcl</code> jobspec.
</summary>

This jobspec deploys a job called "countdash" to a Nomad cluster
running on AWS. You may review the frontend and backend service code in the
[HashiCorp `demo-consul-101`
repository](https://github.com/hashicorp/demo-consul-101/blob/main/services/README.md).

The web application runs on port 9002, so make sure to open that port in your
AWS security group.

If you want to run the job on your localhost Nomad Linux installation with the
`sudo nomad agent -dev` command, you need to update each group's
`service.address` parameter value to `attr.unique.network.ip-address`.


<CodeBlockConfig lineNumbers="true" 
                 filename="countdash.nomad.hcl"
                 highlight="23,27-38,78,82-88,107-116">

```hcl
variable "countdash-api-port" {
  description = "Countdash API Port"
  default = 9001
}
variable "countdash-web-port" {
  description = "Countdash web port"
  default = 9002
}

job "countdash" {

  group "countdash-api" {
    count = 1

    network {
      port "countdash-api" {
        static = var.countdash-api-port
      }
    }

    service {
      name = "countdash-api"
      provider = "nomad"
      port = "countdash-api"      
      address  = attr.unique.platform.aws.local-ipv4

      check {
        name      = "Countdash API ready" 
        type      = "http"
        path      = "/actuator/health"
        interval  = "5s"
        timeout   = "5s"

        check_restart {
          limit = 0
        }
      }          
     }    

    task "countdash-api" {
      driver = "docker"      

      meta {
        service = "countdash-api"
      }

      config {
        image = "hashicorpdev/counter-api:v3"
        ports = ["countdash-api"]
        mount {
          type   = "bind"
          source = "local/application.properties"
          target = "/application.properties"
        }
      }
      template {
        data = "server.port=${var.countdash-api-port}"
        destination = "local/application.properties"
      }
      resources {
        memory = 500
      }
    }
  }

  group "countdash-web" {

    count = 1

    network {
      port "countdash-web" {
        static = var.countdash-web-port
      }
    }

    service {
      name = "countdash-web"
      provider = "nomad"
      port = "countdash-web"
      address  = attr.unique.platform.aws.public-hostname

      check {
        name      = "Countdash web ready"
        type      = "http"
        path      = "/"
        interval  = "5s"
        timeout   = "5s"
      }
    }

    task "countdash-web" {
      driver = "docker"

      meta {
        service = "countdash-web"
      }
      env {
        PORT="${var.countdash-web-port}"
      }

      config {
        image          = "hashicorpdev/counter-dashboard:v3"
        auth_soft_fail = true
        ports = ["countdash-web"]
      }

      template {
        data = <<EOH
        BIND_ADDRESS = ":${var.countdash-api-port}"
{{ range nomadService "countdash-api" }}
COUNTING_SERVICE_URL = "http://{{ .Address }}:{{ .Port }}"
{{ end }}
EOH
        destination = "local/env.txt"
        env         = true
      }
    }
  }
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>
</details>


### Find your deployed services

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="CLI">

Use the [`nomad service list` command](/nomad/commands/service/list) to fetch a list of services.

```shell-script
$ nomad service list
Service Name   Tags
countdash-api  []
countdash-web  []
```

Use the [`nomad service info` command](/nomad/commands/service/info) to fetch
details for a specific service. This example retrieves information about the
`countdash-web` service and formats the output as JSON. Note that the `Address`
value is the public IPv4 address.

```shell-script
$ nomad service info -json countdash-web
[
    {
        "Address": "ec2-3-21-113-32.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com",
        "AllocID": "b1d44744-1592-4241-e904-6a4654a40898",
        "CreateIndex": 849,
        "Datacenter": "dc1",
        "ID": "_nomad-task-b1d44744-1592-4241-e904-6a4654a40898-group-countdash-web-countdash-web-countdash-web",
        "JobID": "countdash",
        "ModifyIndex": 849,
        "Namespace": "default",
        "NodeID": "b348d8ac-1020-1b9a-fbb8-0d4a9dbeb5ca",
        "Port": 9002,
        "ServiceName": "countdash-web",
        "Tags": []
    }
]
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="UI">

1. Access the **Jobs** screen.
1. Click the **countdash** job name. The job details screen opens to the **Overview** tab.
1. Click the **Services** tab to review a list of services deployed by the job.
1. Click **countdash-web** to view the public IP address. 

</Tab>
</Tabs>


## Use service tags

You may specify `service` blocks multiple times with the same name but for
different ports. All instances are returned when the service name is queried.
To restrict results, you may assign [`tags`][] to services to group them. When
querying the service, you may provide tag values as part of the service name.

This example exposes an application on two ports for different protocols.

```hcl
job "..." {
  # ...
  group "..." {
    network {
      port "http" {}
      port "grpc" {}
    }

    service {
      name = "my-app"
      port = "http"
      tags = ["http"]
      # ...
    }

    service {
      name = "my-app"
      port = "grpc"
      tags = ["grpc"]
      # ...
    }
  }
}
```

By assigning different tags, you may reach the port for each protocol with
the `http.my-app` and `grpc.my-app` service queries.

### Canary deployments

When using [canary][jobspec_update_canary] or
[blue/green][jobspec_update_blue_green] upgrades you can specify a different
set of tags for the canary allocations using the [`canary_tags`][]
configuration.

During a deployment, the new allocations are registered with the tags set in
`canary_tags` while non-canaries have the values in `tags`. Having different
sets of tags allow you to create separate [load balancing][learn_lb] routing
rules to preview canaries.

-> **Note**: Services are registered with either `tags` or `canary_tags`. In
  order to share values they must be set in both fields.
